summary: The project has resolved 81 of 118 issues (69%), with all problems closed except for active work concentrated in first_proof_1 (q4, q5, q6). Two problems (q7 at 58%, q10 at 57%) show strong solvability potential, while the remainder cluster in the 9-20% range indicating systemic difficulty. Resource usage today is minimal ($0.19, 2 runs), suggesting underutilized compute capacity relative to the open workload.
problems: q4 is the largest active blocker with 22 open issues against 23 resolved, indicating an unstable or contested proof path
q5 has 14 open vs only 10 resolved — the only problem where open issues outnumber resolved, signaling a stuck or misdirected approach
q8 has the lowest solvability score (9%), suggesting either a misunderstood problem statement or an intractable chosen strategy
Most problems (q1-q6, q9) sit in the 13-20% solvability band, indicating no clear breakthrough path despite resolution counts
Best-proof generation is uniform (100 issues, claude_opus_5-4 across all q1-q10), implying lack of model/strategy diversification
highlights: q7 (58%) and q10 (57%) are by far the closest to solvable and should be prioritized for completion
69% overall resolution rate with 0 in-progress stalls shows the team clears issues to closure rather than letting them linger
All AIM problem lists, Erdos problems, and first_proof_2 are fully resolved, demonstrating capability on well-scoped problems
q6 is nearly complete with only 1 open issue against 15 resolved
Resource cost is extremely low, leaving substantial budget headroom for intensified runs

suggested_todos.title: Close out q7 and q10 first — highest solvability scores and best ROI for completed proofs
Resolve the final open issue on q6 to fully close it
Conduct a root-cause review of q5 where open issues exceed resolved, and reconsider the proof strategy entirely
Re-examine the q8 problem statement and hypotheses given its 9% solvability — likely a misunderstood premise
Diversify proof-generation models/strategies beyond uniform claude_opus_5-4 to break stalls on low-solvability problems
Scale up daily runs to use available budget, focusing compute on the 22 open q4 issues

mistakes: Over-reliance on a single model (claude_opus_5-4) and uniform proof count (100 issues) for every problem, ignoring per-problem difficulty differences
Continuing to generate proofs on low-solvability problems (q8 at 9%, q3 at 13%) without revisiting whether the problem statement or hypotheses were correctly interpreted
Allowing q5 to accumulate more open issues than resolved, indicating a wrong proof direction was pursued without an early pivot
Treating high resolved-issue counts as progress signals while solvability scores remain low — closing sub-issues without converging on a valid overall proof
Underutilizing available compute/budget (only 2 runs today) while 37 issues remain open, slowing momentum on solvable targets like q7 and q10
